source.title: Transcriptome analysis and reverse engineering verification of SNZ3Val125Ile and Pho3Asn134Asp revealed the mechanism of adaptive laboratory evolution to increase the yield of tyrosol in Saccharomyces cerevisiae strain S26-AE2

initial_request.research_problem: Excessive metabolic engineering in S. cerevisiae S26 increased tyrosol but impaired growth, limiting industrial applicability. The goal was to restore growth and further improve tyrosol yield via adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE), then identify and validate the underlying genetic and transcriptomic mechanisms.
initial_request.known_context: S26 is an engineered strain with high tyrosol production but reduced growth rate.
constraints: Industrial applicability requires both high tyrosol yield and robust growth.
quantifiable_goals: Increase tyrosol yield beyond current levels

evolution_trajectory.state_before: S. cerevisiae S26, engineered for high tyrosol, suffers from impaired growth, limiting industrial use.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE) restore growth and further improve tyrosol production in S26?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: ALE can restore growth and further improve tyrosol production in S26.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesis formulated based on known potential of ALE to improve microbial traits.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design ALE protocol to test hypothesis.

evidence.quote_or_span: Although excessive metabolic modification increases the production of tyrosol, it also causes a decrease in the growth rate of yeast.
this study attempted to restore the growth of S. cerevisiae through adaptive evolution and further improve tyrosol production.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that ALE can improve growth and tyrosol production in S26.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to design an ALE protocol that applies selective pressure for improved growth on glucose.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Stepwise increase in glucose concentration will select for mutants with enhanced glucose utilization and growth.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Implement ALE with automatic passaging at OD600=15 and stepwise glucose increase (20→40→80→125 g/L).
evolution_trajectory.observation: Protocol designed to gradually increase glucose concentration over three stages.

evidence.quote_or_span: The initial glucose concentration of the evolution medium was 20 g/L, and the amount of added glucose gradually increased during the process of evolution.

In stage 2, the glucose concentration was increased from 40 to 80 g/L. A total of 20 passages were conducted in this stage, and strain S26-AE2 was obtained. The strain was passaged once every 11.75 h on average
In stage 3, the glucose content increased from 80 to 125 g/L, and the strain was passaged once every 11.95 h on average. The evolutionary strain in stage 3 was named S26-AE3.
In stage 1, the glucose concentration was increased to 40 g/L, and the strain was named S26-AE1. At this stage, the average passage time was 16 h.

SNZ3 contained mutations at amino acid residues 118, 122, and 125; synonymous mutations were identified at amino acids 118 and 122, and a nonsynonymous mutation was identified at amino acid residues 125.

As shown in Table 1, SNZ3 contained mutations at amino acid residues 118, 122, and 125; synonymous mutations were identified at amino acids 118 and 122, and a nonsynonymous mutation was identified at amino acid residues 125.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: ALE protocol designed with stepwise glucose increase.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Will the ALE protocol yield evolved strains with improved growth and tyrosol production?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Evolved strains will exhibit shorter passage times and improved fermentation performance.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform ALE in three stages, isolating strains S26-AE1, S26-AE2, S26-AE3.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Three evolved strains obtained with decreasing passage times: S26-AE1 avg 16 h, S26-AE2 avg 11.75 h, S26-AE3 avg 11.95 h.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Evaluate fermentation performance of evolved strains.

evidence.quote_or_span: In stage 1, the glucose concentration was increased to 40 g/L, and the strain was named S26-AE1. At this stage, the average passage time was 16 h.

In stage 2, the glucose concentration was increased from 40 to 80 g/L. A total of 20 passages were conducted in this stage, and strain S26-AE2 was obtained. The strain was passaged once every 11.75 h on average
In stage 3, the glucose content increased from 80 to 125 g/L, and the strain was passaged once every 11.95 h on average. The evolutionary strain in stage 3 was named S26-AE3.
After the adaptive laboratory evolution of S. cerevisiae S26, three evolutionary strains were obtained.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Evolved strains S26-AE1, S26-AE2, S26-AE3 isolated.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which evolved strain shows the best improvement in growth and tyrosol production?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: S26-AE2 will exhibit the highest biomass and tyrosol yield due to adaptation to 80 g/L glucose.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform fermentation in 100 g/L glucose and compare biomass and tyrosol production.
evolution_trajectory.observation: S26-AE2 achieved 17.82 g DCW/L biomass (15.33% higher than S26) and 817.83 mg/L tyrosol (69.20% higher than S26-AE1).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Select S26-AE2 for transcriptome analysis to understand mechanisms.

evidence.quote_or_span: the biomass of strain S26-AE2 was 17.82 g DCW/L at 48 h, which was 15.33% higher than that of strain S26.
the tyrosol production of S26-AE2 improved significantly, especially at 48 h, and its tyrosol production reached 817.83 mg/L, which was 69.20% higher than the highest yield of strain S26-AE1.
The biomass of strain S26-AE2 reached 17.82 g DCW/L in the presence of 100 g/L glucose, which was 15.33% higher than that of S26, and its tyrosol production reached 817.83 mg/L.
In stage 3, the glucose content increased from 80 to 125 g/L, and the strain was passaged once every 11.95 h on average. The evolutionary strain in stage 3 was named S26-AE3.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: S26-AE2 identified as best performer in fermentation.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What transcriptomic changes underlie the improved growth and tyrosol production in S26-AE2?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Transcriptome analysis will reveal upregulation of central metabolism and downregulation of glucose repression.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform RNA-seq on S26-AE2 vs S26 under 20 g/L and 100 g/L glucose.
evolution_trajectory.observation: In 20 g/L glucose: 36 upregulated, 1 downregulated gene. In 100 g/L glucose: 9 upregulated, 16 downregulated genes. Notably, HXK2 expression decreased under 100 g/L glucose, suggesting reduced glucose repression. Pyruvate synthesis genes (PGK1, GPM1, GPM2, ENO1/ENO2, PYK1) and TCA cycle genes were upregulated.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Identify causal mutations via SNP analysis.

evidence.quote_or_span: Compared with S26, S26-AE2 presented 36 upregulated genes and 1 downregulated gene in the presence of 20 g/L glucose
Compared with the original strain S26, the evolutionary strain S26-AE2 had 9 upregulated genes and 16 downregulated genes in the presence of 100 g/L glucose
The transcriptome analysis revealed that, upon exposure to 100 g/L glucose, the S26-AE2 strain may reduce the transcriptional regulation of glucose repression through decreased HXK2 expression.
we speculated that after adaptive evolution, the decreased expression of HXK2 in strain S26-AE2 reduced the transcriptional regulation of glucose repression in cells

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Transcriptome analysis revealed key expression changes.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which genetic mutations are responsible for the evolved phenotype?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: SNP analysis will identify nonsynonymous mutations in genes related to metabolism.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform whole-genome sequencing and SNP calling on S26-AE2.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 19 SNPs identified, including nonsynonymous mutations in SNZ3 (Val125Ile) and PHO3 (Asn134Asp).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Predict functional impact of mutations via molecular docking.

evidence.quote_or_span: The results revealed 19 SNP mutations in S26-AE2-100vsS26-20, S26-AE2-100vsS26-AE2-20, and S26-100vsS26-20.
five genes with SNP mutations, namely, MAL32, SNZ3, DOT6, HXT3, and PHO3

SNZ3 contained mutations at amino acid residues 118, 122, and 125; synonymous mutations were identified at amino acids 118 and 122, and a nonsynonymous mutation was identified at amino acid residues 125.
PHO3 was mutated at residues 132, 134, and 420, and only amino acid residues 420 exhibited a nonsynonymous mutation.

# Transcriptome analysis and reverse engineering verification of SNZ3 $^{Val125Ile}$ and Pho3 $^{Asn134Asp}$ revealed the mechanism of adaptive laboratory evolution to increase the yield of tyrosol in Saccharomyces cerevisiae strain S26-AE2

evolution_trajectory.state_before: SNZ3 Val125Ile and PHO3 Asn134Asp mutations identified.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do these mutations affect substrate binding affinity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Mutations improve binding to substrates, enhancing enzyme activity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform molecular docking of mutant proteins with substrates.
evolution_trajectory.observation: SNZ3 Val125Ile improved binding to D-ribose 5-phosphate (Vina score -5.7). PHO3 Asn134Asp improved binding to thiamine phosphate (Vina score -6.6).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate SNZ3 mutation effect via reverse engineering.

evidence.quote_or_span: After SNZ3 mutation, the Vina score for the interaction with the small-molecule D-ribose 5-phosphate decreased to -5.7
the interaction of the PRO51, PRO363, MET365, and GLY366 residues with small molecules was reduced, and the binding of the ALA348, ILE398, and CYS408 residues was increased

The docking results showed that after the introduction of the $\mathrm{PHO3}^{\mathrm{Asn134Asp}}$ mutation, its Vina score for the interaction with the small-molecule substance thiamine phosphate decreased to $-6.6$ , which indicated that the affinity between the receptor and the ligand improved (Table S3).
Molecular docking and reverse engineering verified the effectiveness of the SNZ3 $^{Val125Ile}$ mutation in increasing tyrosol production.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Molecular docking suggests SNZ3 Val125Ile enhances substrate binding.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does the SNZ3 Val125Ile mutation alone increase tyrosol production?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Introducing SNZ3 Val125Ile into S26 will increase tyrosol yield.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct S26-SNZ3 Mut strain via CRISPR-Cas9 and test tyrosol production.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Under 100 g/L glucose, tyrosol production by SNZ3 mutant increased by 17.01% compared to control S26 at 72 h.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test double mutant to see additive effects.

evidence.quote_or_span: Under 100 g/L glucose culture, tyrosol production by the SNZ3 mutant increased by 17.01% compared with that of the control strain S26 at 72 h
Reverse engineering demonstrated that a single-nucleotide mutation in SNZ3 could increase the concentration of tyrosol.
Furthermore, the amount of tyrosol produced by strain S26 with the SNZ3 $^{Val125Ile}$ mutation increased by 17.01% compared with that of the control strain S26 following exposure to 100 g/L glucose.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does combining SNZ3 and PHO3 mutations further improve tyrosol?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Double mutant (SNZ3+PHO3) will show additive or synergistic increase in tyrosol.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct S26-PHO3 Mut+SNZ3 Mut and measure tyrosol.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Double mutant increased tyrosol by only 11.37% compared to control S26 at 72 h, less than SNZ3 single mutant.

evidence.quote_or_span: the tyrosol production of the SNZ3 and PHO3 comutants increased by 11.37% compared with that of the control strain S26 at 72 h

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Double mutant underperformed; other SNP genes not tested.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What are the limitations of the current study?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: PHO3 single mutant effect not clearly reported; other SNP genes (MAL32, DOT6, HXT3) not explored; mechanism remains hypothetical without direct enzyme activity measurements.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Acknowledge limitations and suggest future work.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Study validated SNZ3 but PHO3 role unclear; other mutations may contribute; enzyme assays needed.

evidence.quote_or_span: we detected mutations in the key genes PHO3 and SNZ3
Our molecular docking results revealed that mutations in these genes enhance the interaction between proteins and small molecules.

success_verification.final_conclusion: ALE successfully restored growth and increased tyrosol production in S26-AE2. SNZ3 Val125Ile mutation was validated to increase tyrosol by 17.01%, but double mutant with PHO3 did not show additive effect. Transcriptome and SNP analyses provided mechanistic insights, but direct enzyme activity measurements are lacking.
limitations: PHO3 single mutant effect not clearly reported
Other SNP genes (MAL32, DOT6, HXT3) not explored
Mechanism remains hypothetical without direct enzyme activity measurements
